analogy: Think of "Minerals and Energy Resources" through the chapter's own opening images: We use different things in our daily life made from metal. Can you list a number of items used in your house made of metals. You have studied that the earth’s crust is made up of different minerals embedded in the rocks. Various metals are... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: MODE OF OCCURRENCE OF MINERALS, 43 MINERALS AND ENERGY RESOURCES, 44 CONTEMPORARY INDIA – II, 45 MINERALS AND ENERGY RESOURCES. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Minerals and Energy Resources is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that we use different things in our daily life made from metal.

The text explains that can you list a number of items used in your house made of metals.

The text explains that you have studied that the earth’s crust is made up of different minerals embedded in the rocks.

The text explains that various metals are extracted from these minerals after proper refinement.

The text explains that minerals are an indispensable part of our lives.

The text explains that almost everything we use, from a tiny pin to a towering building or a big ship, all are made from minerals.

The text explains that the railway lines and the tarmac (paving) of the roads, our implements and machinery too are made from minerals.

The text explains that cars, buses, trains, aeroplanes are manufactured from minerals and run on power resources derived from the earth.

The text explains that in all stages of development, human beings have used minerals for their livelihood, decoration, festivities, religious and ceremonial rites.

The text explains that haban comes to Guwahati with his father from a remote village.

The text explains that he sees people getting into strange house like objects which move along the road.

The text explains that he also sees a “kitchen” dragging a number of house along with it.

The text explains that he is amazed and asked his father “Why don’t our houses move like the one we saw in Guwahati, Ba?” Ba replies, “These are not houses, they are buses and trains.

The text explains that unlike our houses these are not made of bricks and stones, metal like iron and alluminium are used in making these.

The text explains that they are driven by an engine which needs energy to work.” A bright smile from toothpaste and minerals Toothpaste cleans your teeth.

The text explains that abrasive minerals like silica, limestone, aluminium oxide and various phosphate minerals do the cleaning.

The text explains that fluoride which is used to reduce cavities, comes from a mineral fluorite.

The text explains that most toothpaste are made white with titanium oxide, which comes from minerals called rutile, ilmenite and anatase.

The text explains that dig a little deeper and find out how many minerals are used to make a light bulb?

The text explains that all living things need minerals Life processes cannot occur without minerals.

The text explains that although our mineral intake represents only about 0.3 per cent of our total intake of nutrients, they are so potent and so important that without them we would not be able to utilise the other 99.7 per cent of foodstuffs.

The text explains that geologists define mineral as a “homogenous, naturally occurring substance with a definable internal structure.” Minerals are found in varied forms in nature, ranging from the hardest diamond to the softest talc.

The text explains that rocks are combinations of homogenous substances called minerals.

The text explains that some rocks, for instance limestone, consist of a single mineral only, but majority of the rock consist of several minerals in varying proportions.

The text explains that although, over 2000 minerals have been identified, only a few are abundantly found in most of the rocks.

The text explains that a particular mineral that will be formed from a certain combination of elements depends upon the physical and chemical conditions under which the material forms.

The text explains that this, in turn, results in a wide range of colours, hardness, crystal forms, lustre and density that a particular mineral possesses.

The text explains that geologists use these properties to classify the minerals.

The text explains that study of Minerals by Geographers and Geologists Geographers study minerals as part of the earth’s crust for a better understanding of landforms.

The text explains that the distribution of mineral resources and associated economic activities are of interest to geographers.

The text explains that a geologist, however, is interested in the formation of minerals, their age and physical and chemical composition.

The text explains that however, for general and commercial purposes minerals can be classified as under.

The text explains that mODE OF OCCURRENCE OF MINERALS Where are these minerals found?

The text explains that the term ore is used to describe an accumulation of any mineral mixed with other elements.

The text explains that the mineral content of the ore must be in sufficient concentration to make its extraction commercially viable.

The text explains that the type of formation or structure in which they are found determines the relative ease with which mineral ores may be mined.

The text explains that it is, therefore, important for us to understand the main types of formations in which minerals occur.

The text explains that minerals generally occur in these forms: (i) In igneous and metamorphic rocks minerals may occur in the cracks, crevices, faults or joints.

The text explains that in most cases, they are formed when minerals in liquid/ molten and gaseous forms are forced upward through cavities towards the earth’s surface.

The text explains that major metallic minerals like tin, copper, zinc and lead etc. are obtained from veins and lodes.

The text explains that (ii) In sedimentary rocks a number of minerals occur in beds or layers.

The text explains that coal and some forms of iron ore have been concentrated as a result of long periods under great heat and pressure.

The text explains that another group of sedimentary minerals include gypsum, potash salt and sodium salt.

The text explains that (a) sedimentary rocks (c) igneous rocks (b) metamorphic rocks (d) none of the above (iv) Which one of the following minerals is contained in the Monazite sand?

The text explains that (i) Distinguish between the following in not more than 30 words.

The text explains that (a) ferrous and non-ferrous minerals (b) conventional and non-conventional sources of energy (ii) What is a mineral?

The text explains that (iii) How are minerals formed in igneous and metamorphic rocks?

The text explains that (iv) Why do we need to conserve mineral resources ?

The text explains that (i) Describe the distribution of coal in India.

The text explains that (ii) Why do you think that solar energy has a bright future in India?

The text explains that geological Age of coal found in north east India (8) 5.

The text explains that finest iron ore with magnetic properties (9) 4.

Source: NCERT Class 10 Contemporary India II, Chapter 5: Minerals and Energy Resources

cases.name: Dig
cases.point: Dig a little deeper and collect “Nutritional Facts” printed on food labels. (NCERT Class 10 Contemporary India II Ch5 pg 1)

cases.name: Study
cases.point: Study of Minerals by Geographers and Geologists Geographers study minerals as part of the earth’s crust for a better understanding of landforms. (NCERT Class 10 Contemporary India II Ch5 pg 1)

cases.name: Do
cases.point: Do you know that most of the minerals in India are nationalised and their extraction is possible only after obtaining due permission from the government? (NCERT Class 10 Contemporary India II Ch5 pg 2)

cases.name: But
cases.point: But in most of the tribal areas of the north-east India, minerals are owned by individuals or communities. (NCERT Class 10 Contemporary India II Ch5 pg 2)

cases.name: In Meghalaya
cases.point: In Meghalaya, there are large deposits of coal, iron ore, limestone and dolomite etc. (NCERT Class 10 Contemporary India II Ch5 pg 2)

schemes.name: Do
schemes.point: Do you know that most of the minerals in India are nationalised and their extraction is possible only after obtaining due permission from the government? (NCERT Class 10 Contemporary India II Ch5 pg 2)

schemes.name: Petroleum
schemes.point: Petroleum refineries act as a “nodal industry” for synthetic textile, fertiliser and numerous chemical industries. (NCERT Class 10 Contemporary India II Ch5 pg 10)

prelims_traps: Do not treat MODE OF OCCURRENCE OF MINERALS and 43 MINERALS AND ENERGY RESOURCES as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Minerals and Energy Resources; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Minerals and Energy Resources through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use MODE OF OCCURRENCE OF MINERALS and 43 MINERALS AND ENERGY RESOURCES to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
